core_takeaway: The first position looks advantaged because Agent_1's Quill claim became a protected floor and carried a much higher value than Agent_5's protected Jewel. The last position was not excluded; A5 also got its top item, so the margin reflects floor-setting plus valuation size rather than simple deprivation of the last speaker.
why_or_counterexample: Agent_1 opened by framing Quill as non-negotiable, and later public, private, and formal proposal phases converged on Baseline G with Quill assigned to A1. That gave A1 utility 42.3 versus A5's 27.0. The counterexample is that A5's Jewel floor was also repeatedly protected, and proposals leaving A5 empty were rejected; the first-place edge came because A1's protected item was worth 47 while A5's protected item was worth 30.

phase_notes.public_discussion: A1 first anchored Quill, then the group rapidly converged on Baseline G: A1 Quill, A2 Apple, A3 Stone, A4 Pencil, A5 Jewel, with A1/A3/A5 floors repeatedly named.
phase_notes.private_thinking: Present. All ten private-thinking entries reinforced Baseline G or Track A, treating Quill with A1, Stone with A3, and Jewel with A5 as protected floors while leaving Apple/Pencil as flexible.
phase_notes.formal_proposals: Round 1 had four Baseline G proposals and one A4-favoring deviation; Round 2 all five formal proposals were identical Baseline G, directly preserving A1's and A5's top items.
phase_notes.private_voting: Present. Round 1 voting favored Baseline G but failed unanimity due to Agent_4 parse-invalid rejects; Round 2 all agents accepted all Baseline G proposals.
phase_notes.reflection: Present. Reflections diagnosed Baseline G as the broad consensus and recommended Track A with explicit floor protection and compensation for any deviation.

mechanisms.theme: A1's first claim hardened into a protected floor
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1 used the first public turn to make Quill the central claim, and later private strategy explicitly treated Quill with A1 as non-negotiable. This made the final A1 allocation difficult to dislodge.

evidence.quote: Quill (A1) is the top personal priority and non-negotiable for me.
Keep Quill with A1, Stone with A3, and Jewel with A5 as non-negotiable floors.

mechanisms.theme: Baseline G converted the first claim into consensus
mechanisms.explanation: Once Baseline G became the shared low-conflict frame, the final proposals all kept A1 on Quill. Round 2 then delivered unanimous acceptance of that same allocation.

evidence.quote: Baseline G (A1 Quill, A2 Apple, A3 Stone, A4 Pencil, A5 Jewel)
Baseline G allocation: Quill (3) to Agent_1

mechanisms.theme: The last position also secured a top-item floor
mechanisms.explanation: A5 was not simply crowded out by earlier agents. The last speaker introduced Jewel as their top priority and Baseline G placed Jewel with A5, which the final vote preserved.

evidence.quote: places Jewel with me right away

mechanisms.explanation: The main non-Baseline-G alternative gave A4 Jewel plus Pencil and left A5 empty. That was rejected and later reflected on as a hard block, limiting how far first-mover anchoring could hurt the last position.

evidence.quote: Gives me nothing and deprives me of my top item
leaving any agent with nothing is a hard block
moved Jewel away from A5 and left A5 with nothing

mechanisms.theme: The payoff gap is mostly protected-item value size
mechanisms.explanation: Both first and last positions received their top item under the same final allocation pattern. A1 still beat A5 because Quill was worth 47 to A1, while Jewel was worth 30 to A5, producing 42.3 versus 27.0 after the round discount.
